questions.prompt: A managed-app vendor's docs say `point your domain at app.vendor-host.com`. The client's DNS is hosted at the registrar's default panel (no ALIAS, no flattening, no ANAME). The vendor offers a static fallback IP. What's the path?

choices.label: Force the panel to accept an apex CNAME by editing the zone file directly.
choices.rationale: The apex-CNAME prohibition is from RFCs 1034/2181. You can't bypass it at the zone level.

choices: Use the vendor's fallback IP as an A record at the apex.

choices.label: Tell the client to migrate DNS hosts before the integration can ship.
choices.rationale: Migrating DNS hosts is a bigger conversation than a single integration warrants.

choices.label: Skip the apex and rely only on www.
choices.rationale: Skipping the apex breaks every user who types the domain without www.

questions.explanation: Document the manual-update cost. ALIAS/ANAME/flattening exist at hosts like Cloudflare and Route 53, but not all hosts. When the host doesn't support flattening, the standard fallback is a static A record at the apex, with the maintenance cost of manual updates when the vendor changes IPs.

questions.prompt: A subdomain `mail.example.com` is a CNAME pointing at the mail provider. A client asks you to add an MX record at the same name. What happens?

choices.label: It works; DNS allows CNAME and MX on the same name.
choices.rationale: CNAME is mutually exclusive with all other record types on the same name.

choices: The DNS host refuses it. CNAME on a name is exclusive;

choices.label: It works as long as the targets resolve to the same IP address.
choices.rationale: Target resolution doesn't matter. The exclusion rule is absolute regardless of what the records point to.

choices.label: Only MX and TXT can coexist with CNAME; A and AAAA cannot.
choices.rationale: No record type can coexist with CNAME. The rule applies to all types equally.

questions.explanation: No other types allowed. A CNAME cannot coexist with other record types on the same name, regardless of the types involved. The rule is absolute. Choose CNAME or other records, not both.

questions.prompt: A site is migrating. `www.example.com` was a CNAME to a CDN. The new web host wants a direct A record on www. You add the A and the DNS host rejects it with 'conflicting record type.' What's the fix?
choices: Contact the CDN's support; they're blocking the change.
Delete the existing CNAME on www first, then add the A.
DNS hosts always reject the first record type added on a name; retry.
questions.explanation: CNAME and A can't share a name. The CNAME-vs-other-records rule blocks the combination. Remove the CNAME, then save the A. The CDN doesn't control your DNS host's panel; the rejection is local. DNS nameservers aren't something the tech restarts.
